meta.name: Deep Time Atlas — Great Circle Validation Analysis
meta.description: Test of Alison's Cyclopean Great Circle against 871 UNESCO Cultural World Heritage sites with random baseline.

headline: The Great Circle shows a mild enrichment of UNESCO sites but NOT at statistically significant levels for the tight 50km threshold

interpretation.summary: When tested against the full UNESCO Cultural Heritage dataset, the Great Circle shows 12 sites within 50km (vs 8.0 ± 2.7 random). This is a 1.5x enrichment with Z=1.45 — suggestive but NOT statistically significant (needs Z>2 at minimum, Z>3 ideally).
interpretation.at_500km: At the widest 500km threshold, 105 UNESCO sites fall near the circle vs 82.9 random (Z=2.33). This approaches significance but the wide threshold makes it less meaningful.
interpretation.key_insight: The Great Circle IS enriched for famous ancient sites — Nazca, Easter Island, Persepolis, Petra, Mohenjo-Daro, Cusco, Khajuraho, Preah Vihear all genuinely cluster near it. But the enrichment over random is modest (1.3-1.5x) rather than the dramatic 29x seen in the original 20-site analysis.
interpretation.why_different: The original 29x finding compared 20 hand-picked sites to random points. UNESCO's 871 sites include medieval churches, Roman villas, industrial heritage, etc. — sites chosen for cultural value, not ancient mystery. A fairer test would use ONLY sites older than 3000 BCE.
interpretation.recommendation: The Great Circle finding is the most interesting remaining signal. A proper test needs: (1) A dataset filtered to only pre-Bronze Age sites (>3000 BCE), (2) The full Megalithic Portal database of 25,000-50,000 sites, (3) Testing multiple candidate great circles, not just Alison's
